issue_title: Avoid printing to stderr when exception is caught
issue_body:
My expectation (from Python) is that when I catch an exception, I shouldn't see anything in stderr.

Is there any way to suppress the ``RuntimeError`` being written to stderr in the following case?

```
import curio

async def bad():
    raise RuntimeError('bad task')

async def go():
    async with curio.TaskGroup() as tg:
        task = await tg.spawn(bad)
        try:
            result = await task.join()
        except curio.TaskError as e:
            print('caught the exception', e.__cause__)

curio.run(go)
```

Thanks for curio! This is much easier to use than asyncio.

issue_title: Parser can't handle capital "E" on scientific notation
issue_body:
In a previous version of pint (don't know which) the unit registry parser could handle capital "E" in scientific notation, as in "1E-8".
With the current version it parses correctly "1e-8" but not "1E-8". 
Example:
````
import pint
pint.__version__	# '0.7.2'
ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
1 * ureg('1e-8 m**2/(V*s)')    # works fine
1 * ureg('1E-8 m**2/(V*s)')    # ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: '1E-8'
```
This could be solved by including "E" on the `if` clause on *line 418* of _pint\util.py_, class method `eval_token` of class `ParserHelper`.
`line 418 old:             if '.' in token_text or 'e' in token_text:`
`line 418 new:             if '.' in token_text or 'e' in token_text or 'E' in token_text:`

Best regards,
Bruno

issue_title: MetaPacket changes break pcap's LE classes
issue_body:
_From [jon.ober...@gmail.com](https://code.google.com/u/109518670617871611778/) on July 28, 2007 02:47:02_

The MetaPacket changes in r39 break LEPktHdr and LEFileHdr of pcap.py (and
any other similar classes).  Since the clsdict only contains the
__byte_order__ endianess modifier and inherits the __hdr__ from the base
class, the __hdr_fmt__ won't be set in MetaPacket's __new__.

Patch attached to add a testcase to pcap.py that exercises the issue.

**Attachment:** [dpkt-pcap-endian-test.patch](http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=6)

_Original issue: http://code.google.com/p/dpkt/issues/detail?id=6_

issue_title: Error in serialcommunicator thread when using other threads in application
issue_body:
The serial communicator can cause a lockup.

If there is no data then the run() thread in serialcommunicator can never yield causing the other threads in the user application to never run.

Solution:
Add a time.sleep(0) to the end of the run loop

Could you please cut a new release from HEAD that I can use to create an external wrapper?

Thank you!

issue_title: How does using a multi part index affects the accuracy?
issue_body:
A nanopore read when mapped using minimap2 to the human genome (the whole index is in memory) gives an output as follows.
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12987163/38192839-4333a278-36b2-11e8-88a0-c0422522eed0.png)


When using a multi-part index (I manually split the human genome into 2 pieces and ran minimap2 on each part separately - same as using -I option in minimap I presume), the combined output is as follows.

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/12987163/38192826-319fc712-36b2-11e8-8a28-67b5eb0ae92b.png)



I made the following observations
1. In case of the multipart index, additional mappings are there (alignment score is comparatively low for those and those mappings can be ignored) 
2. There are two primary alignments in the multi-part index. By looking at the alignment scores the actual primary mapping can be deduced (the one with AS 64771 opposed to 18246). For the other mapping (ones with alignment score 18246), it should be possible to deduce whether it is a secondary mapping or a supplementary mapping by looking at the CIGAR strings. 


I have the following two questions.
1. Can the output from a multi-part index be merged such that it is exactly the same as the output for the single whole index? If not is there any reduction in accuracy when the multi-part index is used?
2. Are there any existing scripts/methods to perform this merging? 




How is the mapping accuracy affected when a multi-part index is used rather than a single whole index?

issue_body:
RestartableStrategy has an _add_exception_to_history method, which stores exception objects in a list, which is a subtle way to leak objects in python.

Exceptions contain a reference to the traceback object, which has references to the objects on the stack frame. If a reference is taken to the exception object by another object already in one of the stack frames, a reference cycle is immediately created.

Here is an example, where connection is a Connection with RESTARTABLE strategy, and get it's exception history:

    >>> exception_history = connection.strategy.exception_history

Let's take one of the exceptions from the history:

    >>> exception = exception_history[0][2]

It has a traceback object with a stack frame, lets have a look at its variables:

    >>> exception.__traceback__.tb_frame.f_locals
    {'self': <ldap3.strategy.restartable.RestartableStrategy object at 0x7f9e0fb5bac8>, 'counter': 0, 'unix_socket': False, 'address': [<AddressFamily.AF_INET: 2>, <SocketKind ...

We can see that it has a reference to 'self', which is the very same RestartableStrategy object that we are inspecting, thus it is a reference cycle:

    >>> id(connection.strategy)
    140316845128392
    >>> id(exception.__traceback__.tb_frame.f_locals['self'])
    140316845128392

These leaked stack frames contain references to socket objects, causing severe fd leakage (by default, every unsuccessful connection attempt will create 30 fds that are never closed).

This blog post has a nice writeup about the general issue with taking references to exceptions in Python. 
https://cosmicpercolator.com/2016/01/13/exception-leaks-in-python-2-and-3/

~~One of the possible workarounds can be `del`eting the traceback object from the exceptions, although usually I avoid taking references to exceptions at all cost.~~ edit: you can't delete the traceback from the object.

Avoid wildcard except at the top level of the call stack
issue_body:
The main I/O loops tend to catch all exceptions coming deep from the call stack and re-raise them as `PySnmpError`. As noted in #167, this prevents user callback from raising their own exceptions and making their way up to the main loop.

The current design should probably be changed in a way that pysnmp code should so this exception catching closer to the actual call of an underlying library (and re-raise if needed). But at the top level of the main loop no exceptions should be caught.

issue_body:

```

            raise exceptions.DXFUnexpectedStatusCodeError(response.status_code,

issue_title: chord.mirex produces erroneous results on chords with less than three semitones.
issue_body:
The mirex comparison function incorrectly considers chord spellings that result in fewer than three active semitones, e.g. `C:maj(*3)` or `C:5`; this is the same issue that caused #98, but the fix will need to be different.

The seemingly logical thing to do would be the following, when comparing a reference `R` and an estimation `E`:
* if sum(R.semitones) < 3 and R != 'N', -> -1
* else -> mirex(R, E)

It would be nice if Jedi supported semantic keyword completion. Currently Jedi only lists keywords and adds them to the completion list. This doesn't make sense.

For example:

- `as` should never be able to be completed at the start of a line. (except after a `\`)
- keywords like `continue`, `try`, etc. should only be added after whitespace.

This is the easy part. It's getting more complicated if you want to check if it's possible to write `if`, for example it cannot be written after a bracket `(`, but after a name `x`: `x if foo else 3`.
To do that, we can either write a lot of code by hand or maybe abuse the parser somehow (inluding Parser.error_statements).

Python 2.6 just came to end (2.6.9 was released a few days ago): no more support, bug fixes or security patches.
Python 2.7 is the only 2.x version planned to last before 3.x completely takes over [1].

Most Linux distributions (Ubuntu, Arch Linux, Fedora, etc.) have had Python 2.7 as the default Python 2.x implementation for a long time now. Debian 7 (wheezy), the current stable version, has Python 2.7 as default.
FreeBSD has Python 2.7 as default as well.

Only current RHEL/CentOS stable version are still on Python 2.6 (shame on them).
The old Debian stable version, squeeze, has Python 2.6 as default.

Dropping support for Python 2.6 will have the following benefits:
* Reduce the number of versions to support. This will allow us to focus on writing (modern) code, not on legacy support.
* No more Python 2.6 compatibility issues (str.format(), with statement, etc.).
* Gives us access to argparse (and save a lot of lines of code) [2].
* Remove need for unittest2 dependency (tests are currently broken under Python 2.6).

Feel free to add further benefits or drawbacks as comments.

[1] http://docs.python.org/dev/whatsnew/2.7.html
[2] http://docs.python.org/2/library/argparse.html?highlight=argparse#argparse

I'm interested in using the built-in log capturing and rotation in *upstart* with a *honcho* exported job. I like that you can, if you so desire, specify the folder to which to write logs, but upstart will do it better.

I'm considering a few different ways to do this and wanted to get opinions:
* using `tee` to write to both stdout/stderr and the custom log file; this will have the unfortunate side-effect that you will have the same logs in two places, wasting disk space
* accepting a special value for `--log` to indicate that you want to bypass file logging (maybe `--log -`)
* ignoring the `--log` value in the template; then the script would just write to stdout/stderr and use the default *upstart* logging

I'm leaning toward the last option, even though it disregards the `--log` option and may break existing configurations that depend on the logs being written to a custom folder. I think there's more value in getting access to upstart's log compression and rotating.

Actually, I solved the issue changing the class `BaseVCS` in `__init__.py` as follows:

```
class BaseVCS(object):

    @classmethod
    def commit(cls, message):
        f = NamedTemporaryFile('wb', delete=False)
        f.write(message.encode('utf-8'))
        f.close()
        subprocess.check_output(cls._COMMIT_COMMAND + [f.name], env=dict(
            [i for i in os.environ.items()] + [(u'HGENCODING', u'utf-8')]
        ))
        os.unlink(f.name)

    @classmethod
    def is_usable(cls):
        try:
            return subprocess.call(
                cls._TEST_USABLE_COMMAND,
                stderr=subprocess.PIPE,
                stdout=subprocess.PIPE
            ) == 0
        except OSError as e:
            if e.errno == 2:
                # mercurial is not installed then, ok.
                return False
            raise
```

As you can see, I only changed the params for `subprocess.check_output`:

```
        subprocess.check_output(cls._COMMIT_COMMAND + [f.name], env=dict(

issue_title: Library fails reading long responses , e.g. from the new NRIQSTN command.
issue_body:
Dear,

I was using this library for controlling my TX8150. (Async mode)
All goes well, except for the NRIQSTN command that gives a long response ( > 7000 bytes ) on which the library fails.

The eISCP.get goes wrong. 
It does 

`message = self.command_socket.recv(header.data_size)`

This goes well for all the short responses ( shorter than an ethernet frame )
However for this 7000 bytes response len(message) might be less than header.data_size.
Parsing further down the road goes wrong then ( assertion on EOF fails ).

The quick hack I made, but my skills in the area are rusty, goes as follows


    message = self.command_socket.recv(header.data_size)
    while len(message) != header.data_size :
		ready = select.select([self.command_socket], [], [], 0.5 )
		if not ready[0] :
			break
		ToRead = header.data_size - len(message)
		message = message + self.command_socket.recv(ToRead)

Hi!

We are using the secretstorage module in [one of our tools](https://github.com/ImmobilienScout24/afp-cli). Unfortunately, after the release of version 2.2.0, users of both MacOSX and Ubuntu Linux could not install it via `pip` any more. On both systems, this was caused by the newly introduced dependency on dbus-python, which failed to compile.

From your Travis tests I saw that I need to install `libdbus-glib-1-dev`, and this fixed the problem. I think, however, that the situation before the dependency was added was better:

Before dependency was added:
* works out of the box on Ubuntu & MacOSX
* in case it does not work, install the 'dbus-python' package (Ubuntu has it packaged, no need to use pip)

After the dependency was added:
* does not work out of the box on Ubuntu & MacOSX
* to install on Ubuntu, you need
 * a C-compiler with additional tools (which I happened to have)
 * the package libdbus-glib-1-dev (which might be called differently on other Linux distributions)

issue_title: JSONField form: don't dump if value is already a string.
issue_body:
I have a Django model with a JSONField, and I want to create an instance of it though the admin site. 
In the JSONField I write the following:
```
[
    {"name": "foo"},
    {"name": "bar"}, 
] 
```
As expected, it's an invalid JSON (because of the trailing comma) and a ValidationError is raised.

What is unexpected is that when the form is re-rendered the content of the json field become:
`
" \r\n    [{\"name\": \"foo\", \r\n    {\"name\": \"bar\"}, \r\n]"
`
which is not json valid at all. 

This happens because the `prepare_value` function of the JSONField form is dumping the content of the field, even when it's already a string. 
https://github.com/adamchainz/django-mysql/blob/master/django_mysql/forms.py#L250

Django Version: *Django 1.10.6*

Database and version used: *MySQL 5.7*

issue_title: Django 1.7 warning - You have unapplied migrations
issue_body: Current migration for Django 1.7 is missing related_name argument for the UserSocialAuth.user field. If calling makemigrations command, this creates migration 0002 in social default app. AFAIK related_name shouldn't do any changes in database, so it should be safe to just add related_name=b'social_auth' argument to the CreateModel fields in 0001_initial.py.

**Type: Bug**

**Version**: 0.2.21
**Django**: 1.9.2

**File**: social/pipeline/user.py
**Function**: user_details

**Description**: 
Sometimes user model can store boolean flags obtained from checkbox.
In this case _details_ dict will contain boolean variable (which could be equal to _False_)
In this case the code
`if value and hasattr(user, name):`
will never let the field _name_ to update, because _value_ == _False_
Got a request for LXML support, which would be good for a number of reasons. In Progress.
https://github.com/ponty/PyVirtualDisplay seems cool and maybe helps fixing #1

I thought I'd create this ticket as a tracking ticket for all the swagger 2.0 work.

**Important**:
All version compatibility logic (`if v1.2 do x, else do y`) should  be kept to a single module instead of cluttering the whole project with these switches.  Right now `pyramid_swagger` is well setup for this. All the logic switches can be kept to the `pyramid_swagger.ingest` module.

**Tasks**:
- [x] `pyramid_swagger.ingest.get_swagger_schema(...)` needs to be able to examine the schemas in the `schema_directory`, determine which swagger version it is, and return a `pyramid_swagger.model.SwaggerSchema` loaded with the swagger 2.0 schemas, but which conforms to the same interface as the swagger 1.2 schema.  (Interfaces changes for `api_declarations` addressed in #95)
- [x] api docs validation, should be handled by #81
- [x] `pyramid_swagger.tween.validation_tween_factory` should be largely unchanged, the validators it uses should support the same interface. Required changes are:
  * `cast_params()`  reads the schemas, so it may need an abstraction to work with both versions.
  * `validate_response()` needs to change because `void` doesn't appear to be a type any more
  * `handle_request` reads the body param name from the schema, but it could just be a constant
- [x] `pyramid_swagger.api.register_api_doc_endpoints()` will need to be changed to just return any file in the directory for 2.0. (based on #95)


I think the bulk of the work is really the first task. Once we have an interface that hides the differences between the specs it should be easy to integrate without adding any branches to handled the differences between spec versions.

I successfully got face_recognition working on my Pi 3 following the steps listed here. The "facerec_on_raspberry_pi.py" example works well. Reducing the image size and camera resolution definitely improves performance. I want to do something similar with the "facerec_from_webcam_faster.py" example using the RPi Camera. issue_title: Integrate with --capture and -v options of pytest
issue_body:
There's a [pytest-logging](https://github.com/saltstack/pytest-logging) plugin by @s0undt3ch that provides a nice way to control logging through the default `-v` and `-s` (alias for `--capture=no`) options of pytest-core. The verbosity flag naturally configures the logging level, and the capturing option - whether to output live logs for all tests, or only capture them to show in case of a failure afterwards.

For instance, one can `py.test -vv -s` to see live logs at the `INFO` level or above emitted by all tests.

It would be nice to have something like that in pytest-catchlog.

issue_title: @api.doc can't handle docstrings from decorated functions
Swagger documentation incorrectly contains Sphinx docstring fields
issue_body: The Swagger implementation notes contains numerous Sphinx docstring fields which I presume should be removed, ```python from flask import Flask from flask_restplus import Api, Resource app = Flask(__name__) api = Api(app) @api.route('/my-resource/') class MyResource(Resource): def get(self, id): """ Some summary. :param id: The ID :type id: int :returns: Nothing :rtype: None :raises SomeException: in case of something """ pass if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(debug=True) ``` i.e., per the [parse_docstring](https://github.com/noirbizarre/flask-restplus/blob/85932fbabb9186043d7d03e703ae6f3971fa97ca/flask_restplus/swagger.py#L102) method only the `raises` field is ignored. Note [This](http://stackoverflow.com/a/11315548/1011716) seems like the correct approach for parsing a docstring containing Sphinx formatting. @noirbizarre I'm happy to try to tackle this issue, though I need some additional context. Which Sphinx fields to you care about? Currently the `parse_docstring` method populates only a subset of the Sphinx fields (and defaults `returns` and `params` to `None` and `[]` respectively). Does Swagger leverage these fields elsewhere or is it suffice to merely provide the `summary` (first paragraph) and `details` (remaining paragraphs)? If that's the case the following logic may work, ```python from docutils.core import publish_doctree import xml.etree.ElementTree as ET raw = """ Some summary. This is another paragraph. This is second sentence in the same paragraph. This is a third paragraph. :param id: The ID :type id: int :returns: Nothing :rtype: None :raises SomeException: in case of something """ tree = ET.fromstring(publish_doctree(raw).asdom().toxml()) elems = tree.findall('paragraph') parsed = { 'raw': raw, 'summary': elems[0].text if elems else None, 'details': '\n'.join([e.text for e in elems[1:]]) if len(elems) > 1 else None } ```

issue_title: message.data becoms None in spawned greenlets
issue_body:
Hi there,

I don't have a self-contained test case but I can easily reproduce the following nevertheless in my project.

In a subclass of ```ws4py.websocket.WebSocket``` that is running in a gevent-based server I implemented the ```received_message``` method.

This works fine as long as it looks similar to the below:

```python
def received_message(self, message):
    _self._on_received_message(message.data)
```

However, the moment I spawn the handler function into its own greenlet as below ..


```python
from gevent import spawn

def received_message(self, message):
    spawn(_self._on_received_message, message.data)
```

.. message.data becomes None somewhere between spawn and the moment _self._on_received_message gets called but at any rate _self._on_received_message receives None, the original data is lost by the time that method is called in its own greenlet.

The workaround is to deepcopy it before spawning the greenlet

```python
from copy import deepcopy
from gevent import spawn

def received_message(self, message):
    spawn(_self._on_received_message, deepcopy(message.data))
```

Method added in 0.2.8 release: `build_query_parameters` inside `introspectors.py` does not consider the fact that `paginate_by` attribute is not explicitly defined inside `rest_framework.viewsets.ViewSet` object (It is however explicitly defined inside `rest_framework.viewsets.GenericViewSet`).

Hi,
at first I want to thank you for writing such a nice library. I hope I can contribute to pyroomacoustics in the next months.

I started to write my master thesis in the topic of multi source localisation. I wrote a script derived from your example to see how the different algorithm performance. The scenario is to separate two white noise sources with additive uncorrelated white noise (SNR = 0dB). I was very exited to see how FRIDA performance because in your paper it separated the best. However in the simulation FRIDA had the highest error (see plot). Maybe I didn't configure the used bins right. Do you have any suggestions how to improve the performance? Is the setup maybe wrong?

![figure_1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/9380980/32054445-b6c773ca-ba5e-11e7-81e5-34e37189f957.png)

Output:

> SRP
>   Recovered azimuth: [  72.  109.] degrees
> MUSIC
>   Recovered azimuth: [  70.  110.] degrees
> FRIDA
>   Recovered azimuth: [ 35.62516827  90.06240039] degrees
> TOPS
>   Recovered azimuth: [  68.  112.] degrees


Here is the script:

_example.py_:
```
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pyroomacoustics as pra
from scipy.signal import fftconvolve

#######################
# algorithms parameters
SNR = 0.    # signal-to-noise ratio
c = 343.    # speed of sound
fs = 16000  # sampling frequency
nfft = 256  # FFT size
azimuth_list_degree = [70, 110]
# used frequency bins, dependent on the method
freq_bins = {'SRP': np.arange(30, 129),
             'MUSIC': np.arange(30, 129),
             'FRIDA': np.arange(1, 129),
             'TOPS': np.arange(1, 129)}

###########################################
# We use a UCA-C with radius 4.2 cm and 7 microphones.
# This setup is used by the Amazon Echo
R = pra.circular_2D_array([0, 0], 6, 0., 0.042)
R = np.append(R, [[0], [0]], axis=1)

######
# location of original source
num_src = len(azimuth_list_degree)
azimuth_list = np.array(azimuth_list_degree) / 180. * np.pi

x_length = (nfft // 2 + 1) * nfft
azimuth_length = azimuth_list.shape[0]
mic_signals_part = np.zeros([7, x_length, azimuth_length])
for i, azimuth in enumerate(azimuth_list):
    # propagation filter bank
    propagation_vector = -np.array([np.cos(azimuth), np.sin(azimuth)])
    delays = np.dot(R.T, propagation_vector) / c * fs  # in fractional samples
    filter_bank = pra.fractional_delay_filter_bank(delays)

    # we use a white noise signal for the source
    x = np.random.randn(x_length) / azimuth_length

    # convolve the source signal with the fractional delay filters
    # to get the microphone input signals
    mic_signals_part[:, :, i] = np.array([fftconvolve(x, filter, mode='same') for filter in filter_bank])

# Sum all signal parts to one signal
mic_signals = np.sum(mic_signals_part, axis=2)

# now add the microphone noise
for signal in mic_signals:
    signal += np.random.randn(*signal.shape) * 10**(- SNR / 20)

################################
# compute the STFT frames needed
X = np.array([
    pra.stft(signal, nfft, nfft // 2, transform=np.fft.rfft).T
    for signal in mic_signals])

##############################################
# now we can test all the algorithms available
algo_names = ['SRP', 'MUSIC', 'FRIDA', 'TOPS']
fig = plt.figure(1, figsize=(5*2, 4*2), dpi=90)
i_sps = 1
for i, algo_name in enumerate(algo_names):
    # construct the new DOA object
    # the max_four parameter is necessary for FRIDA only
    doa = pra.doa.algorithms[algo_name](R, fs, nfft, c=c, max_four=4, num_src=num_src)

    # this call here perform localization on the frames in X
    doa.locate_sources(X, freq_bins=freq_bins[algo_name])
    plt.figure(1)
    ax = fig.add_subplot(2, 2, i+1, projection='polar')
    doa.polar_plt_dirac(azimuth_ref=azimuth_list)
    plt.title(algo_name, y=1.08)

    # doa.azimuth_recon contains the reconstructed location of the source
    print(algo_name)
    print('  Recovered azimuth:', np.sort(doa.azimuth_recon) / np.pi * 180., 'degrees')
plt.subplots_adjust(top=0.9,
                    bottom=0.075,
                    left=0.05,
                    right=0.95,
                    hspace=0.4,
                    wspace=0.4)
plt.show()
```

in _doa.py_ the polar_plt_dirac() function has to be adjusted to have this subplot output:
```
        ....
        #fig = plt.figure(figsize=(5, 4), dpi=90)
        #ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection='polar')
        ax = plt.gca()
        ....
```

Thank you for your help! :)

issue_title: TensorTools Randomize Functions could be CUDAfied

It would be nice if we could generate random numbers directly on the GPU as it would presumably be much faster. It seems that tensorflow and pytorch adopt two different methods for this (but seemingly not with Eigen), which can be good references or resources:
Tensorflow (heap-based):
https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorflow/blob/master/tensorflow/core/kernels/topk_op_gpu.cu.cc
PyTorch (radix-selection):
https://github.com/pytorch/pytorch/blob/master/aten/src/THC/THCTensorTopK.cuh

Will it be ok to adapt them into dynet? (Putting the relevant codes in `third_party` with corresponding comments about lisence and sources.)
Maybe something like:
`pair<IndexTensor, Tensor> TensorTools::topk(const Tensor& v, unsigned dim = 0, unsigned k = 1);`
If ok, I would like to work on this.

issue_title: Discussion: app unit publishing timeouts between publisher and router
issue_body:
Changes in the apps, like deployment of new versions or scaling, are not instantly reflated to the routers which results in routers sending traffic to dead containers.

I manage to reproduce this using this simple scenario:
1. Build an app with the example-ruby-sinatra app
2. Make sure that there is 1 web process
2.5 Start a loop, requesting the app url 
3. Scale the down to zero using `deis scale web=0`
3.5 Stop request the app url

The logs collected between steps 2.5 and 3.5 are the following
```
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 200 OK
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
HTTP/1.1 502 Bad Gateway
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Temporarily Unavailable
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Temporarily Unavailable
HTTP/1.1 503 Service Temporarily Unavailable
```

The `200 OK` lines are expected, since the application is running. The `503 Service Temporarily Unavailable` are OK too since we scaled down to zero, thus there are no gateways. But the three `502 Bad Gateway` lines mean that nginx redirects traffic to servers that no longer exist.

INFO boulder-production: config boulder-production-4621fa0 updated

issue_title: Relative paths don't work with charm build in the output flag.
issue_body:
[This](https://github.com/kat-co/elnode-charm/blob/master/Makefile#L4) is effectively using a relative path to direct output to a distribution folder. Through working with @bcsaller @marcoceppi @johnsca and @mbruzek, we discovered that this fails to produce a bin directory in the generated charm.

@bcsaller has asked that I assign this to him.

issue_title: multiple layers that inherit layer-basic gets downloaded multiple times
issue_body:
An example output of my ghost layer https://github.com/battlemidget/juju-charm-ghost which inherits a few layers which all inherit the base layer as their starting point:

http://paste.ubuntu.com/13503577/

Lines 19, 21, 23 show the build process cloning the base layer multiple times. This doesn't break anything but does add an additional wait time when building out the layers

issue_title: Watcher dies with uncatchable ConnectionClosed error
issue_body:
Originally reported via [conjure-up's Sentry](https://sentry.io/canonical-pj/conjure-up/issues/296142667/):

```
ConnectionClosed: WebSocket connection is closed: code = 1006, no reason.
  File "juju/model.py", line 648, in _start_watch
    self.loop)
  File "juju/utils.py", line 94, in run_with_interrupt
    result = [f.result() for f in done if f is not event_task]
  File "juju/utils.py", line 94, in <listcomp>
    result = [f.result() for f in done if f is not event_task]
  File "juju/client/facade.py", line 392, in wrapper
    reply = await f(*args, **kwargs)
  File "juju/client/_client1.py", line 59, in Next
    reply = await self.rpc(msg)
  File "juju/client/overrides.py", line 104, in rpc
    result = await self.connection.rpc(msg, encoder=TypeEncoder)
  File "juju/client/connection.py", line 238, in rpc
    result = await self.recv(msg['request-id'])
  File "juju/client/connection.py", line 175, in recv
    return await self.messages.get(request_id)
  File "juju/utils.py", line 63, in get
    raise value
  File "juju/client/connection.py", line 183, in receiver
    loop=self.loop)
  File "juju/utils.py", line 94, in run_with_interrupt
    result = [f.result() for f in done if f is not event_task]
  File "juju/utils.py", line 94, in <listcomp>
    result = [f.result() for f in done if f is not event_task]
  File "websockets/protocol.py", line 299, in recv
    raise ConnectionClosed(self.close_code, self.close_reason)
```

This should be reported to the Monitor.  However, we probably also need to have `block_until` surface closed connections instead of blocking indefinitely.

issue_title: Track SHA1s for all files in a code folder
issue_body:
Plan:
The AboutCode tool should collect all the sha1's in a directory and put them into a list. 
As a result, when a user uses AboutCode  next time, AboutCode will collect all the sha1's again and compare with this list to track what's been changed.

Related #107 

@pombredanne is this something that we still want to have?

issue_title: virtualenv fails to create virtual environment from Homebrew-installed Python 3.4.3
issue_body:
When I try to create a virtual environment with Python 3.4.3, `virtualenv` errors out:

```
Running virtualenv with interpreter /usr/local/bin/python3.4
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/virtualenv.py", line 23, in <module>
    import subprocess
  File "/usr/local/Cellar/python3/3.4.3_2/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/3.4/lib/python3.4/subprocess.py", line 405, in <module>
    import _posixsubprocess
ImportError: dlopen(/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_posixsubprocess.so, 2): Symbol not found: _PyString_AsString
  Referenced from: /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_posixsubprocess.so
  Expected in: flat namespace
 in /usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/_posixsubprocess.so
```

I've found another reference to the exact same error [on StackOverflow](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/29911318/error-when-setting-up-virtualenv-with-python-3), with the only answer suggesting to add the `--no-site-packages` argument. Looking through `virtualenv -h`, I see that not only is it deprecated, it doesn't change the setting, as its functionality is currently the default. Predictably, this does not help.

issue_title: Only check modified addons in a PR run
issue_body: It isn't useful to check all addons as one has to look at the log if the reason for a failing check is caused by the changed addon or by some other addon.

issue_title: ACS Kubernetes get-credentials fails with a getaddrinfo failed error
issue_body:
I created an ACS Kubernetes cluster via the Azure portal but when I try to use "az acs kubernetes get-credentials" to set up kubectl config I get the following error:

[Errno 11001] getaddrinfo failed
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\main.py", line 33, in main
    cmd_result = APPLICATION.execute(args)
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\core\application.py", line 139, in execute
    result = expanded_arg.func(params)
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\core\commands\__init__.py", line 274, in _execute_command
    result = op(client, **kwargs) if client else op(**kwargs)
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\command_modules\acs\custom.py", line 329, in acs_get_credentials
    '.kube/config', path)
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\azure\cli\command_modules\acs\acs_client.py", line 22, in SecureCopy
    ssh.connect(host, username=user, key_filename=os.path.join(home, '.ssh', 'id_rsa'))
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\paramiko\client.py", line 296, in connect
    to_try = list(self._families_and_addresses(hostname, port))
  File "C:\python27_x64\lib\site-packages\paramiko\client.py", line 200, in _families_and_addresses
    addrinfos = socket.getaddrinfo(hostname, port, socket.AF_UNSPEC, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
gaierror: [Errno 11001] getaddrinfo failed

In the "C:\python27_x64\Lib\site-packages\azure\cli\command_modules\acs\custom.py" it is hardcoded to look for a server in the format of k8s-masters so in my case it tried to connect via: db-kube-k8s-masters.westus.cloudapp.azure.com. But the DNS name created in the portal was db-kubemgmt.westus.cloudapp.azure.com.

It also is hardcoded to try and connect using azureuser as the username. I had created mine with a different name.

issue_title: `az group delete` stalls on active `az disk grant-access` token
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
If an Azure resource group has a VM that has an active disk access SAS token (generated by using `az disk grant-access`), `az group delete` won't be able to delete the resource group. It will spin/attempt indefinitely (or seemingly indefinitely).

Contrast this with a resource group that has active SAS tokens out for things like blobs/files - those don't appear to block a resource group from being deleted

**To Reproduce**

1. Create resource group
1. Create VM, one that has managed disks
1. Grant access to VM disk using `az disk grant-access`, with at least couple hours of time.
1. Attempt to delete the resource group. Observe how this operation will not complete, spinning endlessly.
1. Remove the disk access token (either in Portal or using `az disk revoke-access`). Observe how the resource group deletes itself shortly after.

**Expected behavior**

`az group delete` either deletes the resource group or errors out with approriate message (e.g. `ERROR: Existing disk access found, remove before attempting to delete this resource group`). 

This behavior would be consistent with how Locks work in Azure - If a lock exists the command errors out due to the existing lock.

**Environment summary**
Install Method: shell.azure.com

issue_title: Return 404 on non-existing objects for users with read permissions
issue_body: When using patch and delete on non-existing objects, the users receive a 404 only if they have `write` permissions on the parent, otherwise 403 is returned to prevent leaking information, but as discussed on #915, it also makes sense to return 404 for users with `read` permissions on the parent.

```

You can see there is a `^M` in my `$PATH` which makes me cannot run any executables from `<env>/Scripts`.

We are investigating.

issue_title: source activate sometimes does not empty hash cache in bash
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [X] bug report
  - [ ] feature request


## Current Behavior
<!-- What actually happens?
     If you want to include console output, please use "Steps to Reproduce" below. -->

I have conda 4.3.30.

Sometimes I've noticed when activating an environment that executable locations are not updated to refer to the current environment's bin directory. For example, I just created a new environment and installed the notebook package. `which jupyter-notebook` still gave me a system notebook. I had to empty the zsh hash cache with `hash -r` for zsh to recognize that the conda environment had a `jupyter-notebook` that took precedence over the system one.

My understanding is that resetting the PATH *should* empty the cache, but apparently something didn't work here. I wonder if conda should do `hash -r` when starting a new environment to make sure the cache is empty.

I talked with @fperez once about how conda activates new environments, and he mentioned he had a system where he *always* started a subshell with the new environment. That seems to be a simple solution to cleanly starting and exiting environments.

issue_title: Workflow problem that causes permissions errors
issue_body:
1. Install miniconda as unprivileged user to `~/miniconda`
2. `sudo su - root`
3. Perform conda operation using the above miniconda install, where package download/extract is required

Result is that `~/miniconda/pkgs` gets populated with files that the unprivileged user doesn't have write access to.

In this particular flow, I think what needs to happen is that, in the process of step 3, the `~/miniconda/pkgs` package cache directory is flipped to read-only for the unprivileged user.

So we're going to be doing *something* with the package cache magic file `~/miniconda/pkgs/urls.txt`, but what exactly?  It's probably going to be something like `chown UID:GID ~/miniconda/pkgs/urls.txt` every time you *write* to that package cache.  And the UID part is simple.  How do we know what the best GID is though?  Do we just use the primary of the current user?

issue_title: Possible to store credstash variables in more than one DynamoDB instance?
issue_body:
Hi, 

I'm wondering if it's possible to store credstash credentials in more than one dynamoDB instance? Every variable my team has created is stored in one database, however the read capacity unit requirements are different for some variables as opposed to others. It is not cost effective to increase the dynamo read capacity of the whole instance when only one variable is accessed at high frequencies.

Is there a work around for this?

Thanks,
Marissa

issue_title: cursor.keys is None before calling cursor.stream()
Parameter and Structure learning for continuous data
issue_body: Should let the user specify what kind of distribution the data should be assumed to be coming from.

The current README.md is not interactive . Also it doesn't contain a section refering to our contributing guide for new developers. Apart from it change the dependencies section of python versions , we now have support for 2.7, 3.3 and 3.4 . And add a better guide on how to install. Break the examples section into small parts. Add a section on how to test the code base . At last add which particular lisence we use, also links to tutorials and books etc for probabilistic graphical models. You can take a reference from [scikit-learn](https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn) or [sympy](https://github.com/sympy/sympy) for some of the later points .

issue_title: Theming transformations(?) not applied to reply from plone.subrequest
issue_body:
When calling subrequest() on a clean Plone5 site, the html contains links that resolve to a 404, for example:

issue_title: Test single consumer applicability regeneration endpoint
issue_body:
https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2797

Endpoint to test: http://docs.pulpproject.org/dev-guide/integration/rest-api/consumer/applicability.html#generate-content-applicability-for-a-single-consumer

Suggested steps:
- create and sync a repo
- register consumer
- bind consumer to the repo
- set consumer profile so that at least one rpm which is specified in any erratum and another rpm which is not specified in any erratum have their version less than available in the repo
- trigger applicability regeneration
- fetch applicability results -> there should be 2 rpms and one errata shown as applicable.

Examples for almost all API calls for the steps above can be found here: https://pulp.plan.io/issues/2793#note-3 , just make sure you use the proper endpoint for applicability regeneration, it should be for a single consumer.

issue_title: Headers name and values doesn't accept bytes
issue_body:
Here we have a little problem:

- https://github.com/Pylons/waitress/blob/master/waitress/task.py#L370
- https://github.com/Pylons/waitress/blob/master/waitress/task.py#L374

Running with python2 we are waiting for bytes
Running with python3 we are waiting for unicode string.

We should probably be looking for bytes in both cases and use six.binary_type in that case (http://pythonhosted.org/six/#six.binary_type)

The metaclass abstraction  is leaking sockets, I think.
The mwe is hard to make (but nonetheless attached), but  it's simple to visualise that  the side effect of init, via the side effects of a possibly confusingly named set_persistent (which will connect a socket by default) result in a open socket, that will never be destroyed (until the interactive session is over (or gc zaps) 👀).

```python 
"""
bug tester
"""
import logging
import socket

logging.basicConfig(level="DEBUG")
log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

server = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
server.bind(("", 9997))
server.listen(5)

BUFFER_SIZE = 1400
DEAD = 1


def receive(sock, eof: bytes):
    chunks = []
    recv_more = True
    bytes_recd = 0
    while recv_more:
        chunk = sock.recv(BUFFER_SIZE)
        log.debug("got %s", chunk)
        if chunk == b'':
            logging.info("socket closed")
            return 0, DEAD
        chunks.append(chunk)
        bytes_recd = bytes_recd + len(chunk)
        if eof in chunk:
            recv_more = False

    log.info("recvd %d", bytes_recd)
    return bytes_recd, b''.join(chunks)

while True:
    log.info("start one recv")
    try:
        (client, adresss) = server.accept()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        server.close()
        break

    log.info("client %s", client)

    while True:
        try:
            bytes, msg = receive(client, b"\n")
            if bytes == 0 and msg == DEAD:
                client.close()
                break
            client.send(msg)
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            log.info("Closing")
            break
```

Now create two instruments withe the same name: and watch how many connections appear.
Eventually they may die and get GC, but does seem dangerous.
Possibly also related   to  #411 .

@alexcjohnson

issue_title: Dataset crashes when calling "add_results" if array is too large: "OperationalError: too many SQL variables"
issue_body:
The following code reproduces this error: 

```python
from qcodes import ParamSpec, new_data_set, new_experiment

new_experiment("bug", "buggy")
data_set = new_data_set("none")

x = ParamSpec('x', 'number', label='none', unit='none')
data_set.add_parameter(x)
result = [{"x": v} for v in range(1000)]  # This works with 999
data_set.add_results(result)
```

The issue appears to be in the "insert_many_values" function of the sqlite_base module. The smallest value where this issue appears is 1000. This agrees with the sqlite documentation here: http://www.sqlite.org/limits.html  (see Maximum Number Of Host Parameters In A Single SQL Statement)

A proposed solution is to add results to the database in chunks of sizes 999 or smaller.

issue_title: gluon gpu not working as suggested in docs?
issue_body:
This is still an issue: https://github.com/gluon-api/gluon-api/issues/4

To reproduce, I use the following code which is from the [documentation](https://mxnet.incubator.apache.org/tutorials/gluon/gluon.html), but I replace `mx.cpu(0)` with `mx.gpu(0)`. I have used the Deep Learning AMI and the gpu is indeed working, so my guess is this is a gluon issue.


```
import mxnet as mx
from mxnet import gluon
from mxnet.gluon import nn
from mxnet import nd as F

class Net(gluon.Block):
    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(Net, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        with self.name_scope():
            # layers created in name_scope will inherit name space
            # from parent layer.
            self.conv1 = nn.Conv2D(6, kernel_size=5)
            self.pool1 = nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2,2))
            self.conv2 = nn.Conv2D(16, kernel_size=5)
            self.pool2 = nn.MaxPool2D(pool_size=(2,2))
            self.fc1 = nn.Dense(120)
            self.fc2 = nn.Dense(84)
            self.fc3 = nn.Dense(10)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.pool1(F.relu(self.conv1(x)))
        x = self.pool2(F.relu(self.conv2(x)))
        # 0 means copy over size from corresponding dimension.
        # -1 means infer size from the rest of dimensions.
        x = x.reshape((0, -1))
        x = F.relu(self.fc1(x))
        x = F.relu(self.fc2(x))
        x = self.fc3(x)
        return x
    
net = Net()
# Initialize on CPU. Replace with `mx.gpu(0)`, or `[mx.gpu(0), mx.gpu(1)]`,
# etc to use one or more GPUs.
net.collect_params().initialize(mx.init.Xavier(), ctx=mx.gpu())
data = mx.nd.random_normal(shape=(10, 1, 32, 32))  # dummy data
output = net(data)
```

issue_title: the jassimp TexCoordBuffer  returned in AiMesh are uncorrect
issue_body: the jassimp TexCoordBuffer  returned in AiMesh are uncorrect but positionBuffer and normalBuffer are normal

I am porting Assimp to java, and I saw in the MD2Loader.cpp, function

void MD2Importer::InternReadFile( const std::string& pFile, 
	aiScene* pScene, IOSystem* pIOHandler)

You read the skin name like

// navigate to the first texture associated with the mesh
		const MD2::Skin* pcSkins = (const MD2::Skin*) ((unsigned char*)m_pcHeader + 
			m_pcHeader->offsetSkins);

I discovered with a md2 this is wrong, the offsetSkins are absolute, not relative to the header size

The header size is 17 integers, that is 17*4=68
The m_pcHeader->offsetSkins itself is already 68, so it doenst need to be added to m_pcHeader, the header size

I guess this is valid also for the other offsets

ps: I didnt check on the native Assimp

issue_title: Add tIme-to-commit field in git indexes
issue_labels: [{'id': 267225426, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/chaoss/grimoirelab-elk/labels/enhancement', 'name': 'enhancement', 'color': '84b6eb', 'default': True}, {'id': 858738564, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/chaoss/grimoirelab-elk/labels/good%20first%20issue', 'name': 'good first issue', 'color': 'b60205', 'default': True}, {'id': 267225427, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/chaoss/grimoirelab-elk/labels/help%20wanted', 'name': 'help wanted', 'color': '159818', 'default': True}]
issue_body:
In chaoss/grimoirelab#79 @BenLloydPearson says:

> One thing I have used a lot that doesn't seem to be supported in GrimoireLab is a field that provides the amount of time it takes for code to be committed upstream. This is the difference between the author_date and commit_date fields. In the past, I've done this with a scripted field in Kibana, but from my understanding this is very inefficient, and it also tends to disappear frequently after running Mordred (it seems to do something that wipes out scripted fields when I run it).
>
> Kibana offers a basic time difference calculation, but it outputs a number of milliseconds and needs some formatting to be made human readable. I've typically expressed this in a number of days to the nearest tenth (e.g. 14.2 days), but hours might also work.
>
> Would it be possible to add a field for something like time_to_commit?

I think this is certainly doable and convenient. The value of both author and commit time depend a bit on how the commit is merged, but anyway, this can be useful.

WRT the unit of time, I would make it be seconds, and then use representation to convert it to other units, such as days. But we can discuss that in this ticket.

BTW, pull requests for this are welcome! It shouldn't be difficult to implement this.

Hello!

I'd like to report a possible inconsistency in the Globus Python SDK documentation, compared to the Globus Auth API Reference.

I am working on an application, and part of the application's work involves pulling a person's linked identities.  I am using the Python SDK.  I am also using the Confidential Client, because this is something living on a web server (as opposed to JavaScript, or on a user's system).  I am including "urn:globus:auth:scope:auth.globus.org:view_identity_set" in the list of scopes that I request.

In https://docs.globus.org/api/auth/reference/#verifying_identity_via_openid_connect_id_token Section 3.9, I learned that in order to get a person's complete list of identities (including linked identity UUIDs), I need to do a token introspection, including the "identities_set".

But, in https://globus-sdk-python.readthedocs.io/en/stable/clients/auth/#globus_sdk.ConfidentialAppAuthClient.oauth2_token_introspect, I get the impression that I needed to include "identity_set" ('identity', singular).

In my own testing, it seems that the API Reference docs are correct: When I called `oauth2_token_introspect(TOKEN_HERE, include='identity_set')`, I did not get an identities set.  I tested this with a token attached to my own identity, and I have one linked identity, so I expected an identities set with two members.

Please let me know if you need more information.  Thanks very much!

issue_title: Test cases do not include multiple metablocks with data
issue_body:
Unless my analysis is incorrect, all of the test cases with multiple metablocks have the compressed data contained in just one of those metablocks.
Consequently, the tests don't seem to cover a case where
* backreferences for copy literals span multiple metablocks, 
* p1, p2 refer to literals in a previous metablock,
* distance codes refer to distances in the distance ring buffer that have been inserted in a previous metablock.

That means that erikzenker's PR #258 passes, while I believe it should fail.

{ foo: { IMAGE: 'A' },
  bar: { IMAGE: 'B' },
  test: { IMAGE: 'B' } }
```

Node version: v9.7.1
js-yaml version: 3.11.0

With Django 1.11 and Python 2.7 every time I automatically create migrations a change to the `CompositeForeignKey` is being generated. After some research this seems to be caused by the on_delete attribute being wrapped in a decorator, meaning the on_delete value is never equal.

issue_title: split abstract and derived classes in multiple compilation units
issue_body:
Hi, 

I have a base class with pure virtual functions, and many many derived classes.
Is There a way to have each of the derived classes in a single translation/compilation unit.

I am using code as in the following example.
```
    py::class_<Animal> animal(m, "Animal");
    animal
        .def("go", &Animal::go);

    py::class_<Dog>(m, "Dog", animal)
        .def(py::init<>());

    m.def("call_go", &call_go);
```

The problem is, the derived class "Dog"  takes "animal" as argument.  If i want to put  "Dog" in a single header, how can i get  the "animal" instance.


Greetings Thorsten

Is there a way to use this library to call Python from C++? (as opposed to creating a python module from C++ code)
The *About This Project* section says *"and vice versa"* but the documentation focuses solely on exposing C++ to Python and there are no results if I search for **Py_Initialize** in the source code which could mean I'm out of luck.
That said, maybe it's still posible to do it with some additional code here and there.
If C++ callbacks are supported I would suspect the plumbing is ready for it.

I am testing a workflow but it is really frustrating that

1. A workstation is very powerful (lots of RAM and CPU) but limit on diskspace
2. Another workstation lacks of RAM but has a lot of diskspace

I cannot run the workflow on 1 because it does not have diskspace for all reference genome etc, neither on 2 because it does not have enough memory.

The only way to get around of this problem seems to copy only needed files from 2 to 1, run the command in 1 and copy the result back. This is relevant to our discussion on cluster execution (#406), that is to say, we can probably copy jobs to cluster and submit a job there...

 The option could be called something like `execute_on`, or `run_on`, or `remote`. It should accept a machine name and figure out how to copy files back and forth using `CONFIG[host_name]`.

issue_title: No effective way to stop a lengthy calculation.
issue_body:
Not sure how Jupyter solves this problem but in SoS, if I accidentally evaluate a large matrix, there is no easy way to stop it. Kernel->interrupt will send ctr-C to the subkernel but the Jupyter frontend still displays `*` (busy) and will not accept new inputs.

If `Ctrl-C` has successfully stops the calculation of the underlying kernel, it is a bug in Jupyter or SoS to not able to resume with other commands.

        self._svc_address = 'ipc://' + self.tmpdir + '/services.pipe'

issue_title: linger argument on context.destroy() appears to do nothing
issue_body:
According to the documentation 

    If linger is specified, the LINGER sockopt of the sockets will be set prior to closing.

However, I found that I have to set linger explicitly with setsockopt, or `context.destroy()` will hang indefinitely:

`````
c = zmq.Context()
s = c.socket(zmq.REQ)
s.connect("tcp://127.0.0.1:50000")
s.setsockopt(zmq.LINGER, 0)
print "sending message"
s.send("hello")
print "message sent"
if s.poll(1000):
    print s.recv()
else:
    print "timed out"
print "closing socket"
s.close()
print "destroying context"
c.destroy(0)
print "done"
`````

issue_title: Allow service names to be set in patch call
issue_body:
I have a web app that touches a lot of services, from mysql, redis, memcached, boto, etc.

I also work in a company that has at least one other team using Datadog APM. They've setup their Datadog tracer agent to prefix their services with their project (eg. `myproject-mysql`). This is nice because then services are namespaced, don't collide with other projects' usages, and declutter/organize the [DD APM view](https://app.datadoghq.com/apm/services).

However, the experience to do this with the Python client could be improved.

Right now:

```python
from ddtrace import Pin
from redis import StrictRedis

Pin(service='redis', app='myproject-prod-redis', app_type='db').onto(StrictRedis)
```

Having to import specific modules/objects/classes for each service just to set the app name feels really messy. So I either have to add this code to all the main points where I import various services and clients _or_ do this all in my init module(s).

What I would like:

```python
from ddtrace import patch

MY_SERVICE_PREFIX = 'myproject-prod-'
MY_SERVICE_LIST = ['redis', 'mysql', 'memcached', 'boto']

for service in MY_SERVICE_LIST:
  patch(**{
    service: True,
    name: '{}-{}'.format(MY_SERVICE_PREFIX, service),
  })
```

issue_title: 'NaN'  when using nesterov momentum and high learning rates
issue_body:
**Introduction**
On August 21, I reported a bug on the lasagne group forum about this.
Basically, when you are using nesterov momentum to train conv nets, if your learning rate starts too high, "nans" are generated. In my case, they start happening near epoch 9.

This happens at the most new update of theano and lasagne.

Right now, im using a current version of lasagne that is old but doesnt have that issue : 
"pip install --upgrade --no-deps git+git://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne.git@5a009f98cb479c6c39157027de830a83462dabc0"


Jan.schluete asked me to bisect the code with git to find the guilty commit.
I did it twice to confirm and found it.

_4d4e0b0796634c23ad43889685ee4b428fe30f8a is the first bad commit
commit 4d4e0b0796634c23ad43889685ee4b428fe30f8a
Author: Jan Schlüter <jan.schlueter@    .at>
Date:   Thu Jun 30 13:30:29 2016 +0200

    Have create_param() set the broadcast pattern of created shared variables

:040000 040000 93ce1ab99b4d4bd14d3825a0b143109aa7d234b2 020a514a738f4fd6d29f538fa2d0fdc5af76555b M      lasagne_


The model im using and the learning rate im using are below :  

```
def getModel():
    try:

        input_var = tensor.tensor4('inputs')
        target_var = tensor.fmatrix('targets')

        # Input Layer
        network = lasagne.layers.InputLayer(shape=(None, 1, 7, 7), input_var=input_var)

        # First Convolution Layer
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=60, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=3)
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=60, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=2)
        network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(incoming=network, pool_size=(2, 2))

        # Second Convolution Layer
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=120, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=3)
        network = lasagne.layers.Conv2DLayer(network, num_filters=120, filter_size=(3, 3), stride=1, pad=2)
        network = lasagne.layers.MaxPool2DLayer(incoming=network, pool_size=(2, 2))

        # Hidden Layers
        network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=96)
        network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=32)
        network = lasagne.layers.DenseLayer(lasagne.layers.dropout(network, p=0.5), num_units=1, nonlinearity=lasagne.nonlinearities.sigmoid)

        return network, input_var, target_var

    except Exception as inst:
        print ("Failure to Build NN !", inst.message, type(inst), (inst.args), (inst))
        sys.exit(1)

def getLearningRate(current_epoch=0, max_epochs=1000):
    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 5) / 100:
        return float32(0.150)

    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 10) / 100:
        return float32(0.10)

    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 30) / 100:
        return float32(0.010)

    if current_epoch < (max_epochs * 80) / 100:
        return float32(0.0010)

    if current_epoch <= (max_epochs * 100) / 100:
        return float32(0.00010)
```

Momentum is kept at default.





> If you suspect you have found a bug**, please first try [updating to the bleeding-edge versions of Theano and Lasagne](http://lasagne.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/installation.html#bleeding-edge-version). It may have been fixed already.

**Same issue!**


>   If you are not sure whether the problem lies within your code, Theano, or Lasagne, first post on [our mailing list](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!forum/lasagne-users).

**The problem is in the commit informed above.**


Thanks! I really need this bug fixed since my thesis is using lasagne and referencing it a lot! 

Please help!

class Header:

issue_title: SAM with DeploymentPreference configuration does not create CodeDeploy deployment
issue_body:
I have a simple SAM that, after deployment, in the CloudFormation console shows me the various resources it has created.

![screenshot-us-west-2 console aws amazon com-2017-12-06-04-35-56-200](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2158187/33645015-89dd403e-da83-11e7-8c21-d87ca04d1e8c.png)

As the above screenshot notes, no CodeDeploy "deployment" has been created. When I go to CodeDeploy in the console, I can see the Application and Deployment Group have been created... but Deployment is empty.

CodeDeploy Deployment is where the S3 bucket reference, Lambda details, and other deployment configuration should be referenced. So without this piece, when I upload a new zip to S3, no new Lambda version is created.

Here is my SAM for reference. (The S3 bucket is created in a different CF template and its ARN exported.)

```
AWSTemplateFormatVersion: '2010-09-09'
Transform: AWS::Serverless-2016-10-31
Resources:
  DummyFunction:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
    ¦ FunctionName: BigOnion_dummyFunction
    ¦ Role: arn:aws:iam::XXXXX:role/BigOnion_Lambda_Logs_S3_Dynamo_SNS
  
    ¦ AutoPublishAlias: live
    ¦ DeploymentPreference:
    ¦   Type: AllAtOnce
    ¦   Role: arn:aws:iam::XXXXX:role/LambdaDeploy
  
    ¦ Handler: index.handler
    ¦ Runtime: nodejs6.10
    ¦ CodeUri:
    ¦   Bucket: !ImportValue s3BigOnionFunctionsHolder
    ¦   Key: 'BigOnion_dummyFunction.zip'
```

I have tried this in both us-east-1 and us-west-2 with the same result.

issue_title: DNS label too long message is inaccurate
issue_body:
Steps to reproduce:

`certbot -d .foo.com`

`Requested domain .foo.com is not a FQDN because label  is too long.`

Problems:

Initial label is actually too short (zero length).
Two spaces after `label` and before `is too`.
We should probably say, instead of FQDN, "is not a valid domain name."

Maybe just remove this check altogether? The server does this check pretty early on.

```
    # FQDN checks according to RFC 2181: domain name should be less than 255
    # octets (inclusive). And each label is 1 - 63 octets (inclusive).
    # https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2181#section-11
    msg = "Requested domain {0} is not a FQDN because ".format(domain)
    labels = domain.split('.')
    for l in labels:
        if not 0 < len(l) < 64:
            raise errors.ConfigurationError(msg + "label {0} is too long.".format(l))
    if len(domain) > 255:
        raise errors.ConfigurationError(msg + "it is too long.")
```

issue_title: [Feature] Allow filtering by index creation date
issue_body:
This may work in a large number of cases.  

In some use cases people will be ingesting older data, hence the need for index names based on content data.  But for many (perhaps most) users, indices are created within moments after the UTC rollover.  This provides an opportunity to avoid parsing index names for dates altogether.  Calculating cutoffs could be much easier, and even allow the dreaded `--disk-space` flag to sort exclusively by index creation date, which would fix some aspects of that use case when there are multiple index name patterns.

Similar to https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch/issues/28843, because force merge is a common practice for managing time-based indices and it is often initiated via the curator process, let's also add a warning message here (https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/forcemerge.html) to remind users that they should only be running force merge action against read-only indices.

issue_title: Allow solution+grade cells for free response coding
issue_body:
I often find myself wanting students to freely code and write their own tests in a cell. In the current version I have to create two cells:

1. A solution cell that has my version of the code.
2. A grade cell with `assert True` as a placeholder to grade the students work.

The problem is that I have to create two cells *and* the comments in the formgrader go with the placeholder cell, not the student's code.

If we allow a code cell (like a markdown cell) to be solution+grade, it would be become much nicer to do this type of thing. Also, our current UI allows users to have a solution+grade code cell, so the fact that is doesn't work is some manner of bug.

issue_title: Disable overriding user parameter by uid value
issue_body:
### Description
There must be a way to pass a parameter with the name `user` to the handler when oauth2 security is enabled.

Introducing connexion to an existing project where parameter `user` is used might be problematic. 

### Desired behaviour
There is a configurable name for UID parameter.

### Actual behaviour
Connexion overrides `user` parameter with the content of `uid`
see  `verify_oauth`:
```
            request.context['user1'] = token_info.get('uid')
            request.context['token_info'] = token_info
```

### Steps to reproduce

Based on `examples/oauth2`

app.py:
```
#!/usr/bin/env python3
'''
Basic example of a resource server
'''

import connexion


def get_secret(user, foo) -> str:
    print(f"got {user} {foo}")
    return 'You are: {uid}'.format(uid=user)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    app = connexion.FlaskApp(__name__)
    app.add_api('app.yaml')
    app.run(port=8080)

```

app.yaml:
```
swagger: "2.0"

info:
  title: OAuth Example
  version: "1.0"

paths:
  /secret:
    get:
      summary: Return secret string
      operationId: app.get_secret
      responses:
        200:
          description: secret response
          schema:
            type: string
      security:
        # enable authentication and require the "uid" scope for this endpoint
        - oauth2: ['uid']
      parameters:
        - name: foo
          in: formData
          description: foo param
          required: true
          type: string
        - name: user
          in: formData
          description: user param
          required: true
          type: string
securityDefinitions:
  oauth2:
    type: oauth2
    flow: implicit
    authorizationUrl: https://example.com/oauth2/dialog
    # the token info URL is hardcoded for our mock_tokeninfo.py script
    # you can also pass it as an environment variable TOKENINFO_URL
    x-tokenInfoUrl: http://localhost:7979/tokeninfo
    scopes:
      uid: Unique identifier of the user accessing the service.
```

mock_tokeninfo.* -  is the same as in example.

```
./mock_tokeninfo.py &  
./app.py
curl -X GET --header "Authorization: Bearer 123" -F 'foo=bar' -F 'user=BLA' http://localhost:8080/secret
```

Results in debug output:
```
got jdoe bar
```


### Additional info:

Output of the commands:

- `python --version`
```
Python 3.6.1
```
- `pip show connexion | grep "^Version\:"`
```
Version: 2016.0.dev1
```

### Description
In `connexion/decorators/parameter.py` having `string` mapped to `str` prevents to pass any non ASCII data inside requests parameters even when properly translated inside URL.

Example: I need to filter inside a REST request by some symbol prefix and one of them is 'µ' which is translated in URL to `%C2%B5`:
`curl -X GET --header 'Accept: application/json' 'http://localhost:8200/api/unitprefixes?symbol=%C2%B5'`
This leads to this error:
```

  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/flask/app.py", line 1540, in handle_exception
    reraise(exc_type, exc_value, tb)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/flask/app.py", line 1982, in wsgi_app

Not squishing them together like 3.3. :)

Squishes together.

issue_title: web.run_app(): Terminate properly with signal TERM and INT
issue_body:
## Long story short

This is more or less related to #63. My issue is that `web.run_app()` gracefully exit when SIGINT is sent to the process but not when SIGTERM is sent.

This is because `web.run_app()` catches SIGINT (with `KeyboardInterrupt` exception) but does not override SIGTERM handling (there is no exception raised for that, one can only use `signal.signal`). Most of the time it is SIGTERM that is used to gracefully shutdown a process but that will actually kill the application instead of letting the requests finish properly and response. `kill`, Docker and system.d use SIGTERM by default.

## Expected behaviour

I suppose the default expected behavior of `web.run_app()` is to allow the requests to be finished before terminating the process. That's what most people would think it does. The feeling is also harder since it does it already for SIGINT.

## Actual behaviour

Only SIGINT triggers a graceful exit. SIGTERM does not let the running requests finish.

## Steps to reproduce

1. run this script
```
import asyncio
from aiohttp import web

async def handle(request):
    await asyncio.sleep(60)
    text = "Hello"
    return web.Response(text=text)

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', handle)

web.run_app(app)
```
2. do a GET request on /
3. send SIGTERM to the process
4. you will notice the process ends before the 60 seconds and your request has been canceled

## Your environment

Python 3.6, Arch Linux

issue_title: How can I set the event loop's task factory using the pytest plugin?
issue_body:
## Long story short

I have introduced `aiotask_context` to an existing project in order to implement something like a request context in aiohttp using the middleware feature, which then enabled me to scope SQLAlchemy sessions to the request by using the `scopedsession` factory class. However, doing so made all the aiohttp pytest tests to fail, which was expected but I don't know how to fix them properly as the docs are not clear regarding the event loop (fixtures?) and how to configure it. The application itself runs without any errors when tested manually, the tests however are failing.

## Expected behaviour

All tests passing using the client fixture:

```python
import pytest

from example.app import create_app


@pytest.fixture
async def client(aiohttp_client):
    app = create_app()
    return await aiohttp_client(app)
```

Example test:

```python
import pytest
from tests.common import PING_API_URL


@pytest.mark.usefixtures("client")
class TestGet:
    async def test_get_healthy(self, client):
        response = await client.get(PING_API_URL)
        assert response.status == 200
        text = await response.text()
        assert text == '"pong"'
```

## Actual behaviour

This is the error I get when run the tests:

```
  File "/path/to/Workspace/example-app/example/middlewares.py", line 89, in middleware
    context.set("X-Request-ID", request.headers.get("X-Request-ID", str(uuid.uuid4())))
  File "path/to/Workspace/example-app/.testing-venv/lib/python3.6/site-packages/aiotask_context/__init__.py", line 115, in set
    asyncio.Task.current_task().context[key] = value
AttributeError: '_asyncio.Task' object has no attribute 'context'
```

## Steps to reproduce

Implement the following middleware and database sessions scoping function for your SQLAlchemy Session factory class:

```python
import asyncio
import aiotask_context as context
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker, scoped_session


###
# Middleware
#
async def request_id_middleware(app, handler):
    async def middleware_handler(request):
        context.set("X-Request-ID", request.headers.get("X-Request-ID", str(uuid.uuid4())))
        response = await handler(request)
        response.headers["X-Request-ID"] = context.get("X-Request-ID")
        return response
    return middleware_handler


###
# Scoping function
#
def get_current_request() -> Optional[str]:
    return context.get("X-Request-ID", None)


###
# Application
#
_app = web.Application(
    logger=_logger,
    middlewares=(
        request_id_middleware,
    ),
)


###
# Database w/ SQLAlchemy
#
_db_engine = _create_database_engine()
Session = scoped_session(
    sessionmaker(bind=_db_engine),
    scopefunc=get_current_request)


###
# Running the application
#
loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.set_task_factory(context.task_factory)

app = create_app()

web.run_app(app=app, host="0.0.0.0", port=8080)
```

## Your environment

 * Ubuntu 16.04
 * Pyenv
 * Python v3.6.3
 * Aiohttp v3.1.0
 * aiotask_context v0.5.0
 * SQLAlchemy v1.2.5

issue_title: Client request timeout parameters are inconsistent
issue_body:
## Long story short

The behavior of the `read_timeout` argument of `ClientSession` constructor and the `timeout` argument of `ClientSession.request()` method is inconsistent and prevents use of `ClientSession.read_timeout` in requests by default.

## Expected behaviour

Passing `read_timeout` to `ClientSession` constructor makes the passed value the default timeout for every session request (i.e. when no `timeout` argument is passed to `session.request()`),
as well as passing `timeout=None` explicitly to `session.request()` disables the timeout.

## Actual behaviour

In the docs, it's stated that `timeout=None` passed to `session.request()` disables the timeout. Instead, it forces the use of session's default timeout. 

Moreover, the session's `read_timeout` is effectively unused by default, instead the `DEFAULT_TIMEOUT` (5*60) is used. To enable the session's `read_timeout` for requests, we must pass `timeout=None` explicitly to every request, which in turn contradicts to the meaning of `None` as completely disabling the timeout. 

This behavior is very inconvenient and error-prone, allowing for the situation where we are unexpectedly left without any timeout (when we don't pass a `read_timeout` to `ClientSession` and pass a `timeout=None` to `reqeust()` in order to get the `read_timeout` into effect).

## Steps to reproduce

In this code, timeout is not 0.01 but 5*60:
```
session = ClientSession(read_timeout=0.01)
session.get("https://google.com")
```
In this code, timeout is 0.01 instead of no timeout:
```
session = ClientSession(read_timeout=0.01)
session.get("https://google.com", timeout=None)
```
In this code, there is not timeout at all, if one think naively that omitting `read_timeout` effects in some default timeout being used:
```
session = ClientSession()
session.get("https://google.com", timeout=None)
```

## Your environment

This issue is relevant to any `aiohttp==2.0.7` setup.

```
$ python -We -c "import multidict as x; print(x.__version__)"
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/antoine/miniconda3/envs/dask36/lib/python3.6/site-packages/multidict/__init__.py", line 24, in <module>
    from ._multidict import (MultiDictProxy,
  File "multidict/_multidict.pyx", line 1, in init multidict._multidict (multidict/_multidict.c:14817)
ImportWarning: can't resolve package from __spec__ or __package__, falling back on __name__ and __path__
```

issue_title: Add the remaining property into async timeout
issue_body:
It's useful to know how much time is remaining when scheduling tasks that either do not support asyncio's mechanics or provide an independent timeout.
One such example would be running a subprocess with a program that can accept a timeout:

```python
async with timeout(1234) as t:
    p = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec('iptables', '-w', int(t.remaining), ...)
    await p.wait()
```
